key_insight: A stepped semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC eutectic interface with nanoscale Ni segregation enables strong plastic co-deformation, delaying brittle failure and activating dislocation glide in the hard phase.
engineering_relevance: Designing ultrafine eutectic heterostructures with controlled interface spacing and chemistry could improve strength-ductility balance in additively manufactured structural alloys.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion to create a lamellar Cr-Ni eutectic microstructure (~450 nm spacing)
Atomic-resolution STEM / HR-STEM to resolve interface structure, orientation relationship, and local chemistry
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension plus correlative TEM to track deformation mechanisms
evidence_signal: In situ mechanical testing showed high flow stress with large plastic strains and no cracking, while microscopy linked deformation to dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear, and later BCC dislocation activity.
watch_item: The mechanism is demonstrated on a specific ultrafine lamellar eutectic system, so translation to other alloys or larger structural scales may depend strongly on maintaining similar interface spacing, coherence, and chemistry.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
